speaker: interviewer
text: --is Kurt Klein. And the date is December 7, 1995. Kurt, we left off on the last tape mentioning that you were discharged from the military intelligence with the rank of Second Lieutenant. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your serial number at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can only give you my enlisted and serial number by heart. I can give you the other serial number later. But my former serial number had been 32554510.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. You came back to the United States after having been in Paris and getting married in Paris. Correct? When did you arrived in America with Gerda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was a year after the war, a year which I had spent first in Buffalo where I did in fact find a job in printing and where my friends prevailed on me to stay, rather than going to New York, as I really wanted to do. But I always liked living in Buffalo. And it was only a seeming lack of job opportunities that made me want to try New York, and plus the fact that my sister and brother were living there, of course, also. But basically, most of my friends that I had made before going in the Army were living and still some relatives were living in Buffalo. So that's where I did settle down. I went into the printing business and stayed with a printing firm until I decided it was time to put an end to all this red tape that kept Gerda and me apart and that I would go to Paris where she was living by that time. That is what I did. We managed against almost insurmountable obstacles to get married because we were both aliens in France. And you needed to have a certain length of residence there. And we ran into every conceivable type of catch-22. For instance, they didn't want to issue me a visa in New York because in Europe when you get married they post banns at the city hall, which means that anybody who has any objection to this marriage can come and make his objections. So they told Gerda they wouldn't post those banns until she could show that I had a visa. And the French consulate in New York told me that he wouldn't issue a visa before the banns were posted in Paris. This was just one example of the type of thing we would run into. But nevertheless, we overcame all this and got married June 18, 1946. There was still a period of waiting for word from the Turkish consulate because Gerda had an uncle in Istanbul who wanted us to go from Paris to Istanbul so he could, first of all, see her again after all she had gone through and lost the entire family and wanted to meet me, of course. But the Turks strung us along. They would have let me go to Istanbul or give me a visa. But they strung us along. They weren't sure about Gerda, who was at that time still considered a Polish citizen. So we spent a couple of months in Paris only to be told in the end that the visa been refused for Gerda. So we came back late in August of '46 first to New York where we had a religious ceremony-- having had a civil ceremony in Paris-- with my family and then went to Buffalo where a friend of mine had found an apartment, which was a very difficult thing in those days with all the returning service people. But nevertheless, we had had an apartment. And eventually I found another job in printing because I had to give up my previous job in order to go to France. And so we gradually settled in. And of course, Gerda was something of a novelty, if not a sensation, because in those days most of the displaced persons were still in Europe and not many had come. Gerda had been able to come faster than the others because she was my wife. But she had time to fit into the mainstream of that life. And she gradually made the necessary adjustments. And we established a circle of friends. Of course, Gerda's dream had always been to have a family. And this being '46, it took until '48 when we had our first child, a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vivian. And that, of course, helped Gerda in every way, as I'm sure she will tell you. And so we began a way back to normalcy and to some sort of a life. And Gerda was, of course, welcomed by all. I still had some older relatives who sort of served as surrogate relatives for Gerda as well. And four years later, we had our second daughter, Leslie. And that was in '52. And by '57 our son was born and those are the three children we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Jim.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you learn the fate of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we made inquiries after the war. To be exact, in March of '46, we found out through a tracing agency in Paris. It had nothing to do with my going to Paris. I believe that we had written letters because that was the agency to go to. And they had compiled a record in so far as these names and numbers were known. And they were able to confirm that on the 19th of August, 1942, my parents had been sent from the camp in the south of France to a transit camp by name of Drancy, from which all French Jews eventually were deported. All the ones who were deported were deported from that camp-- and that they had been deported on the 19th of August and that their lists did not show that they had been repatriated later, so that they had to be assumed dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years did you remain in Buffalo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, myself, lived a total of 48 years in Buffalo. And we left in 1985 after I retired from my printing firm. Actually, I started a printing firm in '48 of my own, which when it came time for retirement I was able to sell. And one of the conditions was that I would stay with the job to service my former accounts. And so I stayed with the firm, a larger firm than mine, that took me over, and stayed with them to take care of what I had always done in my own firm until 1985 when I was 65 and I knew it was time to retire. And that's when we came to Phoenix, to Scottsdale, to be exact.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the meantime, of course, we had had grandchildren. Our eldest daughter Vivian had a girl, a boy, and a girl. And she lives here in the Phoenix area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband's name is Jim, Jim Ullman. And our second daughter, who is Leslie Simon, married Roger. And while they lived here in Phoenix at one time for a couple of years, they now live in Las Vegas where he is an ophthalmologist. And our son Jim-- I forgot to mention that they have three daughters, Julie, Melissa, and Jessica.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first daughter's, Vivian's children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: --are Elissa, Andrew, and Lindsay. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jim is married to who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jim is married to Lynn. They have two daughters, Jennifer and Alexa, and live in the Washington, DC area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we end, I want to ask you a few questions. What became very important to you after the war was over? You mentioned to me you had a goal. You and Gerda had a goal in particular. After having lived through what you did, what was that goal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't so much a specific goal as it was that we knew, first of all, that we wanted to bring our experiences, as much as we could do, to others. And of course, Gerda, early on, after in fact coming out with her book, which is her autobiography was asked to speak, and very naturally and spontaneously gravitated into speaking and bringing her message to others and incidentally, the things to guard against and what are some of the experiences that she distilled from what she had to go through. And by and large, I, myself was rather busy establishing a business and running it and didn't have much time for that. But in more recent years, I too have spoken to a number of audiences around the country. Gerda has always written about those experiences. And I, being something of an editor, could help her with that and also write my own experiences. And on retirement, I knew I had one project that needed to be done. And that was to-- I had this, as I mentioned, collection of 400 letters from my parents plus all the peripheral letters dealing with the authorities in our frustrated attempts to rescue our parents. And I knew that most of those letters were written in German, of course. And I knew that for them to mean anything to our family and to their descendents, I had to translate them. And so I set to work translating as many letters from my parents as a could. And a whole picture of the gradual deterioration of their situation emerges from those letters, in fact. And because this was initially only meant to be a family chronicle to be used by our children, and theirs, and so on-- but PBS got wind of the fact that I had those letters. And they were looking for someone with exactly the type of experience that I had in trying to rescue people from Europe. And they then made their documentary on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were both a survivor and a liberator. How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, while I was, of course, fortunate and was spared the very worst that I later was to see, I nevertheless have enough of an insight of what was happening on the other side. I always knew what my parents, for instance, were up against and that there was only a very, very slim chance that we would ever get them out alive. And as I said, I knew Nazi mentality. I felt them capable of the very worst from the very early beginning. I knew that they, with certainty, that they were capable of anything, including the genocide long before anybody else believed that there would be genocide. Because the German Jews in general, as I said, deluded themselves into thinking that nothing like that was ever meant by the Nazis, and that perhaps if they were patient enough that the conditions for them would eventually straighten out and they would have some sort of a life there. That's what kept too many of them there when they could still have gotten out. But so I understood what we were dealing with. I had no illusions about that. What it has done for me is it has given me a few warning signs. When I see certain things happening that you can't ever be too complacent about, certain unrest in a country, or when economic conditions are turned bad, that somebody doesn't have to find a scapegoat, which we experienced in our own bodies, which were, of course, Jews. And there's enough anti-Semitism still around so that I think the Jews can always be found to be made to be scapegoats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was invited to go back, but I gratefully declined it because I did not want to sit down with those people after what had happened. And I also could not imagine myself being forced to sit down, and be civil, and drink with them, and hear how they all love Jews, and they each had a Jew they protected, and that they never knew what was going on. I simply couldn't take that, because in fact after the war, I had experienced that on another level, that no German ever knew what was going on, no German was ever a Nazi. And I'd like to know what happened to those hundreds of thousands who raised their arms in their heils at the party congresses in Nuremberg and all the others who were marching constantly. So I didn't want to have to deal with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you keep in touch with anybody that you were with in your unit in the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To this day you still keep in touch?

speaker: interviewee
text: To this day. Yes. We have occasionally seen each other. Some of them, a number of them, live in the New York area. And we have seen some of them this year, in fact. There are others, whom I met at Army reunions. And every so often somebody else surfaces that I had long forgotten and in some fashion has heard of this and contacts us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What advice would you like to give to your children, your grandchildren, future generations? After being both a survivor and a liberator, you've seen a lot. You've experienced a lot. What advice would you like to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say to them, first of all, that they should beware of fanatics on any side of the spectrum. I think they should question authority in so far as it goes against their principles. I think that the majority, just because it is a majority also, is not necessarily a right, that might does not necessarily make right. Examine the circumstances. And if something goes against your principles and beliefs, don't blindly follow it as I have seen, just hundreds of thousands follow it, and millions. And I have seen the results of what intolerance coupled with fanaticism can do. Basically, as a liberator, I'm also grateful that I was spared the very worst. Both of us together consider that what was given to us by us being able to meet is really a gift of life and that we want to make the most of that gift. And what I would also tell to our children and to everybody is that be aware of the plight of others. And try to help them, because you're not only doing it for them. Ultimately, you're doing it for yourself as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. I'd like to bring in your wife now to introduce her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Kurt, before you introduce your wife Gerda, I'd like to ask you one last question. In the interrogation of the prisoners of war, were there some more difficult prisoners that it was hard to get information out of? And what means would you use in order to get that information?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, you have found many who were initially, at least, quite difficult and didn't want to talk. But we had some subtle psychological methods that seemed to work quite effectively. And what we would do in a case like that is, if we had a group who didn't talk much or not at all, we would segregate them from the rest of the other prisoners in an obvious place where they could see the other group. And then we would pin large signs on them that said Russia. And that was one thing that none of them wanted, is to fall into Russian hands. That was to be avoided at all costs. And that usually worked quite effectively.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, I would like you to introduce your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with great, great pleasure, here is the girl I stumbled across under those circumstances and who has changed the course of my life considerably, all for the better. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we certainly are grateful that we had the chance to meet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gerda, would you like to say something to Kurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a good deal that I have said for the past almost 50 years. And I hope I'll have the privilege for a few more years. Kurt not only opened the door for me and this incredible morning to freedom, but to that I have quality of life and which makes the healing of some wounds possible. For everything that I am and I've had the privilege of doing, I credit Kurt with that. And I don't think we need to say it publicly. We know it. And we have been much blessed. And we feel that we have an enormous debt to pay to others who have not been as fortunate. And we have tried to do something about that. And Kurt has always been extremely supportive of that. It's nothing that you can do that is really worthwhile that you do alone without the support of your husband and your children. And I've had the good fortune to get that in abundance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I find that through Gerda, I've really found a real purpose in life. And some of it we are realizing even now in being able to bring a certain message to people. And we certainly know each other's feelings. And I don't think we need to spell them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: No we don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my parents, my father Ludwig Klein, my mother Alice Klein, taken by me in the vicinity of Heidelberg on one of the mountains, probably in 1936, one year before I left Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you take this picture with you to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took it with me. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my parents soon after they got married. It's my father, Ludwig Klein and my mother Alice Klein. It was presumably taken in 1910 in Heidelberg, where they lived at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This came to me because it was in my sister's possession. And when she passed away, it came to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is the picture on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture on the left is one of my mother with my brother and sister, my mother Alice Klein, my brother Max Klein, and my sister Irmgard Klein, taken, as far as I can determine, during the war years of the First World War at a time at which they lived in Heidelberg. The picture on the right is a baby picture of me probably taken in 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Waldorf, where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture on the left and the girl on the left is my sister Irmgard Klein, later Ehrlich, with two of her friends from school in Heidelberg, taken-- very difficult to say-- but 1932, perhaps. And the picture on the right is a picture that was most likely taken in 1935 of my sister, center in the front row going through nurse's training in Stuttgart, and as I said, probably in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Want to start? Who's the picture on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture on the left is one of me while we, the fifth division, were in the city of Metz. And in the background is the armory in which we were quartered. The picture in the middle is that of a bridge that the Third Army put up as we went across the Sauer River. And as you can perhaps see, words to the effect that this is by courtesy of the fifth division and allows you to go into Germany. The third picture on the right is taken in Berchtesgaden, in which Hitler had a retreat up on a mountain called the Berghof. And this was the large conference room in which so many official pictures were taken. And that is probably me standing in the window getting a view of all the Nazi hierarchy saw from up there. It was taken in the summer of 1945 on a visit to Berchtesgaden.
